methods.method_name: Tree-Ring Data Collection and Chronology Development
methods.description: Process for collecting tree-ring samples, processing them, cross-dating, measuring, quality control, detrending, and combining into a standardized chronology for dendroclimatological analysis.

steps.description: In June 2009, Chinese pine tree-ring samples were collected from three different sites in the Lingkong Mountain area (Fig. 1).The first sampling site (112 • 5.227 E, 36 • 35.588N, 1480-1700 m a.s.l.) was in the national nature reservation park of Lingkong Mountain, labeled LKS, where 60 treering cores from 30 living trees were extracted using the increment borer.The second site (112 • 5.20 E, 36 • 46.38 N,  1450-1650 m a.s.l.) was located to the northwest of LKS, and labeled WJW, 40 tree-ring cores from 20 living trees were collected from this area.At the third site (112 • 24.62 E, 36 • 53.97 N, 1450 m a.s.l.), trees older than 100 yr are very difficult to find, so only 8 cores from 4 old healthy trees were collected, this site was named JF.The distance between the three sites was greater than 30 km.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect tree-ring cores from specified sites using an increment borer
inputs: Study sites (LKS, WJW, JF) coordinates and altitude

steps.description: Tree-ring samples were processed according to the standard dendrochronological technique (Cook and Kairiukstis, 1990).
steps.searchable_summary: Process tree-ring samples according to standard dendrochronological techniques

steps.description: The Skeleton-plot cross-dating method (Stokes and Smiley, 1968) was adopted to preliminarily assign the calendar years to each growth ring.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Skeleton-plot cross-dating to assign calendar years to growth rings
outputs: Cross-dated tree-ring samples with preliminary calendar years

steps.description: All the tree rings in each core were measured to the nearest 0.01 mm.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure tree-ring widths with high precision (0.01 mm)

steps.description: The COFECHA programme (Holmes, 1983) was utilised to estimate the quality of cross-dating and ring-width measurements.
steps.searchable_summary: Use COFECHA program for quality control of cross-dating and ring-width measurements

steps.description: Series with short ages or that were abnormal in comparison with the majority of series were discarded from the chronology construction.
steps.searchable_summary: Discard low-quality or abnormal tree-ring series based on quality assessment
outputs: Filtered set of tree-ring series for chronology construction

steps.description: Because the COFECH results showed that strong similarities existed among the ring-width series from different sites, we finally combined all of the samples into one group to produce a regional chronology.
steps.searchable_summary: Combine tree-ring series from different sites into a single group based on similarity
inputs: Filtered tree-ring series from multiple sites

steps.description: The tree-ring width chronology was developed using the ARSTAN program (Cook, 1985).
steps.searchable_summary: Develop tree-ring width chronology using ARSTAN program

steps.description: To retain as much long-term climate variance as possible, negative exponential curve or straight line with negative slope was applied to each tree-ring measurement series to remove the non-climate trends related to tree age or the effects of stand dynamics.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply detrending (negative exponential or straight line) to remove non-climate trends from tree-ring series

steps.description: We divided the raw data of each ring width by the corresponding year's value of the fitted curve to give a dimensionless index.
steps.searchable_summary: Index detrended tree-ring series by dividing by the fitted curve value

steps.description: Finally, all individual indices were combined to produce a standard STD chronology by means of "biweight robust mean".
steps.searchable_summary: Combine individual tree-ring indices using biweight robust mean to produce a standard STD chronology

steps.description: A subsample signal strength (SSS) threshold of 0.85 (Wigley et al., 1984) was applied to assess the reliable starting year of the chronology, excluding the low quality of earlier years due to low sample size.The signal strength of the chronology was also evaluated over time using statistics of the calculated running series of average between-tree correlations (RBAR) (Briffa and Jones, 1990) and the running express population signal (EPS) (Wigley et al., 1984) based on a 50 yr window.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess chronology reliability using SSS (threshold 0.85), running RBAR, and running EPS (50 yr window)

methods.description: Application of various statistical methods to analyze climate-growth relationships, reconstruct past climate (PDSI), verify reconstruction fidelity and stability, test spatial/temporal representativeness, and identify periodicities in the reconstructed series.

steps.description: Climate-growth relationships were investigated using Pearson correlation analysis between the tree-ring chronology and the meteorological records as well as the Dai-PDSI data.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Pearson correlation analysis between tree-ring chronology and climate data (meteorological records, Dai-PDSI)

steps.description: A simple linear regression model was adopted to reconstruct the mean PDSI value from March to August.
steps.searchable_summary: Develop a simple linear regression model to reconstruct mean March-August PDSI

steps.description: The fidelity of the reconstruction was verified by comparison with the Dai-PDSI data
steps.searchable_summary: Verify reconstruction fidelity by comparing reconstructed PDSI with Dai-PDSI data

steps.description: and checked by the split calibration-verification method (Meko and Graybill, 1995).
steps.searchable_summary: Check reconstruction fidelity using the split calibration-verification method

steps.description: Furthermore, the stability of the regression model was also tested by applying the Bootstrap (Cook and Kairiukstis, 1990) ... statistical methods, which have been adopted in dendroclimatology (Liu et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Test regression model stability using the Bootstrap statistical method
outputs: Bootstrap statistics (e.g., confidence intervals for coefficients)

steps.description: ... and Jackknife (Efron, 1979) statistical methods, which have been adopted in dendroclimatology (Liu et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Test regression model stability using the Jackknife statistical method
outputs: Jackknife statistics (e.g., estimates of bias, variance)

steps.description: The temporal and spatial representativeness of the PDSI reconstruction was tested by comparisons with other tree-ring-based moisture indexes from nearby area
steps.searchable_summary: Test temporal and spatial representativeness of PDSI reconstruction by comparing with other nearby tree-ring moisture indexes
inputs: Other tree-ring-based moisture indexes from nearby areas
outputs: Comparison results (e.g., correlation with other indexes)

steps.description: and spatial correlation analysis between the Dai-and reconstructed March-August PDSI and the PDSI grid dataset according to KNMI Climate Explore (http://climexp.knmi.nl).
steps.searchable_summary: Test temporal and spatial representativeness by performing spatial correlation analysis between reconstructed/Dai-PDSI and a PDSI grid dataset
inputs: PDSI grid dataset (e.g., from KNMI Climate Explore)

steps.description: Multi-taper spectral analysis (MTM) (Mann and Lees, 1996) was conducted to identify the periodicities in the reconstructed series.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct Multi-taper spectral analysis (MTM) to identify periodicities in the reconstructed series
